title: AI SaaS Workflows
lesson:
SaaS stands for Software as a Service — software you rent over the internet instead of installing, like Notion, Slack or Shopify. An AI SaaS workflow is the chain of steps that runs inside such a product every time it uses a model to do real work: take the user's request, gather the right context, call a model, check the result, and ship it back — reliably, cheaply, and at scale.

This topic is about the plumbing around the model, not the model itself. The hard part of a real AI product is rarely the clever prompt; it's everything that has to happen before and after.

Two analogies: the kitchen and the assembly line

Think of a busy restaurant. The chef (the model) is brilliant, but a restaurant is not just a chef. There's a host who takes the order (your API), a runner who fetches ingredients from the pantry (retrieval), a recipe the chef follows (the prompt), a quality check before the plate leaves the pass (validation), and a bill at the end (billing and usage tracking). A great chef in a chaotic kitchen still ships cold, wrong food. AI SaaS is mostly kitchen design.

A second picture: it's an assembly line. The raw part (user input) moves down a belt, each station adds or checks something, and a defective part gets pulled before it reaches the customer — not after.

The standard request lifecycle

Almost every AI feature, from a support bot to a contract summariser, follows the same skeleton:

  1. Authenticate & rate-limit. Who is this user, are they paying, and have they made too many requests this minute? (Rate-limiting means capping how often someone can call you, so one user can't drain your whole budget.)
  2. Gather context. Pull the user's documents or history, often via RAG, so the model answers from real data, not memory.
  3. Build the prompt. Combine a fixed system prompt (the standing instructions) with the user input and retrieved context.
  4. Call the model, often letting it use tools to look things up or take actions.
  5. Validate the output — is it valid JSON? Did it refuse? Is it safe?
  6. Return & record. Stream the answer to the user and log tokens, cost and latency.

Here is that skeleton as code. Notice how little of it is the actual model call:

async def handle(req):
    user = authenticate(req)            # who & are they paying?
    rate_limit(user)                    # stop one user draining the budget
    context = rag.search(req.text)      # fetch real facts
    prompt = build_prompt(SYSTEM, context, req.text)
    out = model.call(prompt, tools=TOOLS)
    result = validate(out)              # JSON valid? safe? retry if not
    log_usage(user, out.tokens, out.cost)
    return result

Why workflows, not one giant prompt

Beginners try to do everything in a single mega-prompt. Real products break the job into small, checkable steps, which is the heart of agentic workflows. Want to extract data from an invoice?

  • Step one pulls the fields.
  • Step two checks the numbers add up.
  • Step three reformats into your database shape.

Each step is cheap to test, easy to swap, and fails loudly instead of silently. A common pattern is routing: a tiny, cheap model (an SLM, a small language model) classifies the request first, then sends easy ones to a cheap model and hard ones to an expensive one. This is the single biggest lever on cost.

The three things that actually break

In production, three things bite hardest:

  • Cost: every token costs money, so cost-per-token math decides whether your product is profitable.
  • Reliability: models are non-deterministic (they can give different answers to the same input), so you need retries, timeouts, and a fallback model for when your main provider is down or slow.
  • Structure: the rest of your app needs clean data, so you force the model to return strict JSON and validate it, treating a malformed reply like any other failed network call.
Toy demoProduction SaaS workflow
One prompt does everythingMany small, testable steps
Trusts the model's outputValidates, retries, has fallbacks
Ignores cost & speedRoutes by difficulty, caches, tracks spend
Breaks silently on weird inputFails loudly with logs & alerts

💡 Treat the model as one unreliable microservice among many. You'd never trust a flaky third-party API blindly — wrap it the same way: timeout, retry, validate, fall back, log.

Master this and you can build a real business on top of models you don't even own. The model is the engine; the workflow is the whole car around it that makes it safe to drive.
mentalModel: The model is the chef; an AI SaaS workflow is the whole kitchen around it — host, pantry runner, recipe, quality check at the pass, and the bill. A brilliant chef in a chaotic kitchen still ships cold, wrong food, so most of the work is kitchen design: gather context, build the prompt, call the model, validate, and record cost.
takeaways: The model is the easy part; the workflow around it is the real work.
Break jobs into small, testable steps — never one giant prompt.
Wrap the model like a flaky API: validate, retry, fall back, log cost.
mistakes: Cramming everything into one giant prompt. A single mega-prompt is impossible to test or debug. Break the job into small steps — extract, then check, then format — so each one fails loudly and can be fixed in isolation. This is the core of agentic workflows.

Trusting the model's output blindly. Models are non-deterministic and sometimes return broken JSON, refusals, or nonsense. Always validate the shape, and treat a bad reply like a failed API call — retry it, don't ship it.

Ignoring cost until the bill arrives. Every token is money. Without routing easy requests to a cheap model and tracking cost-per-token, a feature that demos beautifully can lose money on every single user.

No fallback when the provider is slow or down. Your model API will have outages and latency spikes. A production workflow needs a timeout and a backup model or graceful error, or your whole product goes dark with it.
Forgetting rate limits and abuse. One user (or a bot) hammering your endpoint can burn your entire monthly budget in an hour. Authenticate, cap requests per user, and watch for runaway loops.

Skipping logs and evals. If you don't record inputs, outputs, tokens and latency, you're flying blind — you can't tell if a prompt change made things better or worse without offline evaluation on real examples.
exercise:
Sketch (on paper or in code) the full workflow for a 'summarise this support email and draft a reply' feature. List every step from request to response: authenticate the user, rate-limit them, fetch the customer's past tickets as context, build the prompt, call the model asking for strict JSON like {"summary": "...", "draft": "..."}, validate that the JSON parsed, and log the token cost. For each step, write one sentence on what happens if it fails and what you do about it.

Stretch goal: Add a routing step at the front: a tiny cheap model first labels the email as 'simple' or 'complex', and only complex ones go to the expensive model. Estimate, with rough numbers, how much cheaper this makes 1,000 emails a day if 70% are simple.
solution: The point is to see how thin the model call is compared to the scaffolding. A solid answer has the model call sitting in the middle, wrapped by auth + rate-limit before it and validate + log after it, with a retry loop around the call itself (broken JSON? ask again once, then error cleanly). For each failure: auth fails → 401; rate-limit hit → 429 'try again later'; context fetch fails → proceed with less context or apologise; model times out → retry then fall back; JSON won't parse → retry with a stricter instruction, then return a safe error. For the stretch goal, suppose the expensive model costs roughly 10× the cheap one. Sending all 1,000 emails to the expensive model = 1,000 units. With routing, 700 simple ones cost ~0.1 each (70 units) and 300 complex ones cost 1 each (300 units) — plus the tiny classifier — landing near 370–440 units, well under half the cost. That gap is exactly why routing is the first thing teams add.
summary: An AI SaaS workflow is the plumbing around the model: authenticate and rate-limit the user, gather context (often via RAG), build the prompt, call the model with tools, validate the output, then return and log it. The model is the easy part — production work is breaking jobs into small testable steps, validating and retrying outputs, routing easy requests to cheap models, and tracking cost, speed and reliability. Treat the model as one unreliable microservice: wrap it with timeouts, fallbacks, validation and logs.

keyTerms.def: Software you rent and use over the internet rather than installing on your own machine, like Notion or Slack.
The fixed chain of steps a request passes through — gather context, call the model, validate, return — usually built from small, checkable stages.
The journey of a single user request from arriving (auth, rate-limit) through the model call to the validated, logged response.
Capping how many requests a user can make in a window of time so one person can't drain your budget or overload the system.
Using a cheap classifier first to send easy requests to a cheap model and hard ones to an expensive model, the biggest lever on cost.
A backup plan — a second model or a safe error message — for when your main model provider is slow, down, or returns garbage.
Checking the model's output is the right shape and safe (e.g. parses as the expected JSON) before the rest of your app uses it.
Giving possibly different answers to the same input each time, which is why workflows need retries and validation rather than blind trust.

safe?</text><rect class="dg-box" x="410" y="212" width="150" height="56" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="485" y="237" text-anchor="middle">Retry / fallback</text><text class="dg-sub" x="485" y="255" text-anchor="middle">on bad reply</text><rect class="dg-box dg-box-good" x="80" y="212" width="150" height="56" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="155" y="237" text-anchor="middle">Return & log</text><text class="dg-sub" x="155" y="255" text-anchor="middle">tokens, cost</text><text class="dg-note" x="360" y="310" text-anchor="middle">A bad reply loops back to retry or a fallback model before it ever reaches the user.</text></svg>
diagram.caption: A user request flows through auth, context-gathering, a thin model call, and validation — and a bad reply loops back to retry or fallback before being returned and logged.

quiz.q: In a production AI SaaS feature, what is usually the smallest, easiest part of the workflow?
options: The actual call to the model
Gathering the right context to send
quiz.explain: The lesson stresses that the model call is thin — most engineering effort goes into the auth, context, validation, retry and logging that wrap it.

quiz.q: Your feature demos beautifully but loses money on every user. Which workflow change is the biggest single lever on cost?
options: Asking the model to be more concise in the system prompt
Routing: a cheap classifier sends easy requests to a cheap model
Adding more example documents to every prompt
Streaming the response instead of returning it all at once
quiz.explain: Routing sends the many easy requests to a cheap model and reserves the expensive model for hard ones — described as the single biggest lever on cost.

quiz.q: The model returns text that won't parse as the JSON your app expects. What does a well-built workflow do?
options: Pass the raw text downstream and hope it works
Crash the request so the user retries manually
Treat it like a failed API call: retry with a stricter instruction, then fail safely
Switch permanently to a different model for all users
quiz.explain: Malformed output is treated like any failed network call — validate the shape, retry once with a stricter prompt, and return a clean error if it still fails.

quiz.q: Why do production workflows need a fallback model and timeouts around the main model call?
options: To make the answers more creative and varied
Because model providers have outages and latency spikes that would otherwise take your product down
To reduce the number of tokens in the prompt
Because fallbacks are required by the JSON spec
quiz.explain: Models are an unreliable dependency — providers go slow or down — so a timeout plus a backup model or graceful error keeps your product running when theirs fails.
